content.problemStatement:
We have a rectangular piece of cardboard with width 111 (its height is not important). We are going to fold it in the following way:


pick up the right side of the piece of cardboard and move it to the left until it touches the left side
this way, a smaller piece of cardboard with width 1/21/21/2 is created; if we unfolded it, we could see a vertical ridge formed in the middle
pick up the left side of this new piece of cardboard and move it to the right until it touches the (new) right side
pick up the right side of the resulting piece of cardboard and move it to the left until it touches the left side, etc.
Whenever the cardboard is folded, exactly one of its new sides is a newly formed ridge (there may be more, internal ridges formed, but we do not consider these). Let's denote such a ridge created in the kkk-th folding by RkR_kRk​.

In total, we fold the piece of cardboard NNN times. Afterwards, we unfold it and look at the formed ridges. Let's denote the distance of ridge RNR_NRN​ (i.e. the last formed outer ridge) from the left side of the original piece of cardboard by DND_NDN​. For example, D1=1/2D_1 = 1/2D1​=1/2 and D2=1/4D_2 = 1/4D2​=1/4.

It is possible to express DND_NDN​ as an irreducible fraction x/yx/yx/y. Find this fraction.

Assume that it is possible to fold the piece of cardboard as many times as we want.
content.inputFormat: The first and only line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. For each test case, a space and an integer N follows.
content.outputFormat: Print a single line containing 2T space-separated integers. For the i-th test case (1≤i≤T), the 2i−1-th and 2i-th integer should denote x and y — the position of the last ridge as an irreducible fraction.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, we only fold once, so x = 1 and y = 2. For the second test case, we fold the piece of cardboard twice, so x = 1 and y = 4.

hidden.label: edge case
hidden.expectedOutput: This input is invalid, as N must be greater than 0

solution.approach: To find the position of the last ridge, we can use a simple iterative approach. We start with the initial width of the cardboard, which is 111. Then, for each fold, we calculate the new width by dividing the current width by 2. The position of the last ridge is then calculated as the sum of the widths of all the folds, which can be represented as a geometric series. We can use the formula for the sum of a geometric series to calculate the position of the last ridge.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T*N) — where T is the number of test cases and N is the number of folds. The time complexity is linear with respect to the number of test cases and the number of folds.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — the space complexity is constant, as we only need to store a few variables to calculate the position of the last ridge.

code.Python3:
T = int(input())
for _ in range(T):
    N = int(input())
    x = 1
    y = 1
    for _ in range(N):
        y *= 2
    print(x, y, end=' ')
